content.full_text:
EVELLE J. YOUNGER
ATTORNEY GENERAL

STATE OF CALIFORNIA

S2015

OFFICE OF THE ATTORNEY GENERAL

Department of Justice

A98311

555 CAPITOL MALL, SUITE 350
SACRAMENTO 95814
(916) 445-9555

September 1, 1978

Misuse of State Funds – Standards

TO: Elected Officials, Department Heads
and Administrative Officers

Two years ago this office circulated a letter discussing
the problem of misuse of public resources in political
campaigns. We believe that effort was helpful. The
discussion which follows is a reminder of the potential
for problems in this area.

The general rule is:

"That a public office is a public trust
is a universal truism. No trustee thereof may
with impunity exploit its prerogatives,
embezzle its properties, or appropriate its
funds." (People v. Marby (1942) 51 Cal.App.2d
759.)

Recent cases applying this general rule to the question of
resources use in political campaigns are: Stanson v. Mott
(1976) 17 Cal.3d 206; People v. Sperl (1976) 54 Cal.App.3d
640; and People v. Battin (1978) 77 Cal.App.3d 635.

In general, these cases support the proposition that use
of public resources to support or oppose any candidate
or to support or oppose any measure submitted to the electors
for approval is improper. The principle involved was
summarized in Stanson in this manner:

"A fundamental precept of this nation's demo-
cratic electoral process is that the government
may not 'take sides' in election contests or
bestow an unfair advantage on one of several
competing factions." (Stanson, supra at 217.)

TO: Elected Officials, Department Heads
    and Administrative Officers
Page 2


These cases, as well as older cases in the area, condemn
the use of personnel time, paper, telephones, photocopv
or other reproduction methods, office space, etc. to
advance a political interest. Illustrative of the types
of actions which are improper is the listing contained in
the recitation of facts in Hattin (see pages 643-646).
Any use of public paid time or publicly owned materials
for (1) addressing campaign newsletters, (2) mailing
newsletters and other campaign materials, (3) preparing
lists of precinct polling judges, (4) preparing and sending
press releases, (5) delivering and picking up campaign items
to or from printers and florists, (6) reviewing and preparing
campaign disclosure statements required by law, (7) making
telophone calls relating to fundraisers, (8) accompanying
the candidate at campaign events, (9) preparing telephone
messages, (10) typing thank you notes to campaign supporters,
(11) copying campaign materials on publicly owned photo-
copiers, (12) obtaining voter lists, (13) preparing and
promoting campaign events including fundraisers, and
(14) soliciting campaign contributions would be improper.

When violations occur, remedies may be sought by public
agencies or taxpayers. Potential penalties include felony
prosecution, personal civil liability and sanctions which
could lead to loss of position.

It is important to emphasize that this is a developing and
complex area about which it is fairly said that you should
seek legal advice from your counsel if you have any questions
about any proposed activity.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        EVELLE J. YOUNGER
                                        Attorney General

                                        [illegible]
                                        CHARLES A. BARRETT
                                        Chief Deputy Attorney General

CAB/JAC:vdb

sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an Attorney General advisory letter, not an FPPC advice letter despite the document ID format. No Q/C sections present. Document provides guidance on misuse of public resources in political campaigns citing Stanson, Sperl, and Battin cases. Contains detailed list of 14 types of prohibited activities.

The general rule is: "That a public office is a public trust is a universal truism. No trustee thereof may with impunity exploit its prerogatives, embezzle its properties, or appropriate its funds." (People v. Harby (1942) 51 Cal.App.2d 759.) Recent cases applying this general rule to the question of resources use in political campaigns are: Stanson v. Mott (1976) 17 Cal.3d 206; People v. Sperl (1976) 54 Cal.App.3d 640; and People v. Battin (1978) 77 Cal.App.3d 635. In general, these cases support the proposition that use of public resources to support or oppose any candidate or to support or oppose any measure submitted to the electors for approval is improper. The principle involved was summarized in Stanson in this manner: "A fundamental precept of this nation's democratic electoral process is that the government may not 'take sides' in election contests or bestow an unfair advantage on one of several competing factions." (Stanson, supra at 217.) TO: Elected Officials, Department Heads and Administrative Officers These cases, as well as older cases in the area, condemn the use of personnel time, paper, telephones, photocopy or other reproduction methods, office space, etc. to advance a political interest. Illustrative of the types of actions which are improper is the listing contained in the recitation of facts in Battin (see pages 643-646). Any use of public paid time or publicly owned materials for: (1) addressing campaign newsletters, (2) mailing newsletters and other campaign materials, (3) preparing lists of precinct polling judges, (4) preparing and sending press releases, (5) delivering and picking up campaign items to or from printers and florists, (6) reviewing and preparing campaign disclosure statements required by law, (7) making telephone calls relating to fundraisers, (8) accompanying the candidate at campaign events, (9) preparing television messages, (10) typing thank you notes to campaign supporters, (11) copying campaign materials on publicly owned photocopiers, (12) obtaining voter lists, (13) preparing and promoting campaign events including fundraisers, and (14) soliciting campaign contributions would be improper. When violations occur, remedies may be sought by public agencies or taxpayers. Potential penalties include felony prosecution, personal civil liability and sanctions which could lead to loss of position. It is important to emphasize that this is a developing and complex area about which it is fairly said that you should seek legal advice from your counsel if you have any questions about any proposed activity. Very truly yours, EVELLE J. YOUNGER Attorney General CHARLES A. BARRETT Chief Deputy Attorney General

embedding.summary: A September 1, 1978 letter from the California Attorney General's office to elected officials and administrators reminding them that use of public resources (personnel time, materials, equipment, office space) to support or oppose candidates or ballot measures is improper under California law, citing relevant case law and providing examples of prohibited activities.
